macro_segments.title: The Foundation of Belief in a Single Witness (Eid Echad) for Yibum: Investigation-Based vs. Inherent Testimony Credibility

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Can a single witness testify to allow yibum when the husband dies?
Two Alternative Explanations for Believing Eid Echad: Either the fact that matters naturally come to light (milta d'avida l'igluyei) or the woman's own investigation (dayka u'minsva)
Tosafot's Concern: Why does the Gemara present these as alternatives when Rishonim already assumed the investigation-based explanation?

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing Cases: Single Witness for Husband's Death vs. Brother-in-Law's Death

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Case of Overwritten Testimony: How the order of deaths affects yibum obligation and suggests belief in a single witness
The Critical Question: Can we believe a single witness to permit the sister-in-law (yevama) to marry another man without yibum?
Woman's Own Testimony: She can testify her husband is dead (for marriage or yibum) but cannot testify the brother-in-law is dead

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Incentives: Why the Sister-in-Law Might Not Investigate Properly When Hating the Brother-in-Law

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's Analysis: Hatred is a more powerful force than love in motivating negligence, with worse consequences for the woman
The Asymmetry Explained: Love of the brother-in-law leaves alternatives; hatred traps her in an unwanted marriage

macro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet's Resolution: The Mishna Proves Nothing About Single Witness Credibility

micro_segments.title: Even the Woman Herself Cannot Testify to the Brother-in-Law's Death: This parallels the question about a single witness
The Gemara's Alternative Reading: The Mishna Cannot Serve as Proof Because It Talks About Two Witnesses, Not One
The Gemara's Rejection of Rav Acha Bar Minyumi's Proof: Even if the second witnesses are 'eidei hazama' (refuting witnesses), the Mishna is about overturned facts, not discredited witnesses

macro_segments.title: The Principle of When a Single Witness Is Believed: Tied to Whether the Person Is 'Trapped' (Agunah Concern)

micro_segments.title: When is a Single Witness Believed? Only when Someone's Status Is Trapped and Needs Relief
Why Not Believe a Single Witness About the Brother-in-Law's Death? The Man Is Never Trapped; Only the Woman Benefits
Rava's Kal Vachomer Argument: If we believe a single witness for the grave issur of karet (death-penalty transgression), surely for the lighter issur lav (single violation)?

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Final Analysis: The Woman's Own Credibility Determines the Single Witness's Credibility

micro_segments.title: The Objection to Rava: The Woman Herself Is Not Believed About the Brother-in-Law, So We Should Not Believe a Single Witness Either
Why Is the Woman Not Believed About the Brother-in-Law? Because She Might Hate Him and Will Not Do Due Diligence (lo dayka u'minsva)
If the Single Witness's Credibility Is Bolstered by Her Investigation, Then We Cannot Believe the Single Witness When We Cannot Believe Her

macro_segments.title: Transition to the Mishna: Scenarios of Remarriage and the Status of Children from Unauthorized Unions

micro_segments.title: First Case: When the Husband Shows Up Alive After the Woman Did Kiddushin with Another Man
Rabbi Elazar's Drasha on 'Ve-Ishah Grushah Me-Ishah': A Woman Divorced from Her Husband Only
Second Case: Wife Goes to Foreign Country, Man Marries Her Sister Based on False Report of Her Death
Third Case: When the Wife Actually Dies After the Man Married Her Sister
Rabbi Yosi's Principle: Anyone Who Invalidates Through Others Invalidates Through Themselves; Vice Versa

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Expansion: Wife and Brother-in-Law Both Go Overseas; Both Reported Dead

micro_segments.title: Scenario Setup: The Man Marries the Sister Based on Reports of Both Wife and Brother-in-Law's Deaths
The Irony: The Sister-in-Law (Yevama) Suffers All Negative Consequences When the Reports Prove False, But the Original Wife Remains Permitted to Her Husband
Proof That the Marriage Was Real Enough: The Yevama Requires Divorce from Both Her Husbands; Why Not Also Forbid the Original Wife?

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's Position: Even Sister-in-Law and Brother's Wife Require Get in Certain Circumstances

micro_segments.title: The Challenge: If the Sister-In-Law Suffered Consequences, Why Doesn't the Original Wife Also Become Forbidden?
The Proof: A Baraita Lists Cases Where Get Is Not Required (Blood Relations), Then Where Rabbi Akiva Adds Sister-in-Law and Wife's Sister
Rabbi Akiva's Addition: Even Sister and Brother's Wife Need Get When It Looks Like They Were Married

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Severe Limitation of Rabbi Akiva: Get Is Only Required If the First Marriage Was Kiddushin Without Nisuin

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda Explains Rabbi Akiva's Case: The Brother Had Only Done Kiddushin, Not Nisuin, So It Might Be Negated
The Logic: Because Kiddushin Can Fall Through on a Condition (Tnai), People Might Think It Never Took Effect
Wife's Sister Case: Similarly, If Only Kiddushin Happened to the First Wife, the Marriage to the Sister Is Seen as Real and Requires Get
But Not After Nisuin: Once Nisuin Occurred, Even Rabbi Akiva Agrees No Get Is Required Because No One Would Question the Validity of Nisuin

topical_tags.term: Milta d'Avida l'Igluyei (Matters Come to Light Naturally)
Hatred vs. Love (Sinas Hasone vs. Ahavas Ha'Ohev)
Chiluf Hayu Ha-Devarim (Order of Deaths Was Reversed)
